parts.content: Determine if the composite function $(f \circ g)(x)$ has an inverse for $x\in \mathbb{R}$.
parts.markscheme:
- Since this is a quadratic function opening upward, it fails the horizontal line test <marks>R1</marks>

- Therefore, $(f \circ g)(x)$ is not one-to-one and has no inverse function over all real numbers <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Form the composite function
steps.content:
To determine if the inverse exists, we first need to find the algebraic equation for the composite function $(f \circ g)(x)$. This is defined as $f(g(x))$.

Substitute the expression for $g(x)$ into $f(x)$:

steps.title: Simplify the expression
steps.content:
Substitute $$g(x) = x^2 - 1$$ into $$f(x) = 2x + 3$$ and simplify:

$$\begin{aligned} (f \circ g)(x) &= 2(x^2 - 1) + 3 \\ &= 2x^2 - 2 + 3 \\ &= 2x^2 + 1 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Analyze the function type
steps.content:
The resulting function $$(f \circ g)(x) = 2x^2 + 1$$ is a **quadratic function**. 

Since the domain is all real numbers ($$x \in \mathbb{R}$$), the graph is a parabola opening upwards. For a function to have an inverse, it must pass the **horizontal line test**.

gdc.instructions: Plot y = 2x^2 + 1. Notice it is a U-shaped curve.

ti84.instructions: Press Y=, enter 2x^2 + 1 into Y1. Press GRAPH. Observe the parabola shape.

calculator.description: Visualize the function

steps.title: Apply injectivity test
steps.content:
Quadratic functions on the real domain are **many-to-one**. This means different inputs can produce the same output.

For example, if we evaluate at $$x=1$$ and $$x=-1$$:

$$\begin{aligned} (f \circ g)(1) &= 2(1)^2 + 1 = 3 \\ (f \circ g)(-1) &= 2(-1)^2 + 1 = 3 \end{aligned}$$

Since distinct $$x$$ values map to the same $$y$$ value, the function fails the horizontal line test.

steps.title: State the conclusion
steps.content: Because the function is not one-to-one, it **does not have an inverse** for $$x \in \mathbb{R}$$.

steps.title: Find composite function
steps.content:
First, we need to find the expression for the composite function $(f \circ g)(x)$. We do this by substituting $g(x)$ into $f(x)$.

Given $f(x) = 2x + 3$ and $g(x) = x^2 - 1$:

$$ (f \circ g)(x) = f(g(x)) = 2(x^2 - 1) + 3 $$

Expanding and simplifying:

$$ (f \circ g)(x) = 2x^2 - 2 + 3 = 2x^2 + 1 $$

steps.title: State condition for inverse
steps.content:
For a function to have an inverse, it must be **one-to-one** (injective). This means that every unique input $x$ must produce a unique output $y$.

Using the **Counterexample Method**, if we can find two different input values that produce the same output, we prove the function is not one-to-one and therefore has no inverse.

steps.title: Test distinct inputs
steps.content:
Since $(f \circ g)(x) = 2x^2 + 1$ is a quadratic function involving an $x^2$ term, let's test a positive and a negative value of $x$ to see if they yield the same result.

Let's calculate the output for $x = 1$ and $x = -1$.

gdc.instructions: Calculate 2(1)^2 + 1

ti84.instructions: Enter 2(1)^2 + 1 to find f(g(1))

calculator.description: Evaluate the function at x=1 and x=-1 to check for one-to-one property

steps.title: Calculate outputs
steps.content:
Substituting our chosen values into $(f \circ g)(x) = 2x^2 + 1$:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{For } x &= 1: & (f \circ g)(1) &= 2(1)^2 + 1 = 3 \\ \text{For } x &= -1: & (f \circ g)(-1) &= 2(-1)^2 + 1 = 3 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.content:
We have found that two distinct inputs ($x=1$ and $x=-1$) produce the same output ($3$).

Since the function is **not one-to-one**, $(f \circ g)(x)$ does **not** have an inverse for $x \in \mathbb{R}$.

highlights.text: not one-to-one and has no inverse function

steps.title: Find the composite function
steps.content:
First, we need to find the algebraic expression for the composite function $(f \circ g)(x)$. We substitute the inner function $g(x)$ into the outer function $f(x)$.

$$\begin{aligned} (f \circ g)(x) &= f(g(x)) \\ &= f(x^2 - 1) \\ &= 2(x^2 - 1) + 3 \\ &= 2x^2 - 2 + 3 \\ &= 2x^2 + 1 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Sketch the graph
steps.content: Now we sketch the graph of $y = 2x^2 + 1$. This is a quadratic function. Since the coefficient of $x^2$ is positive ($2 > 0$), the graph is a parabola that is **opening upward**. The vertex is at $(0, 1)$.

steps.title: Apply horizontal line test
steps.content:
To determine if a function has an inverse, we use the horizontal line test. If any horizontal line intersects the graph more than once, the function is not one-to-one and cannot have an inverse.

Looking at our graph of $y = 2x^2 + 1$, a horizontal line at $y=3$ intersects the curve at two distinct x-values:

$$\begin{aligned} 3 &= 2x^2 + 1 \\ 2 &= 2x^2 \\ 1 &= x^2 \\ x &= \pm 1 \end{aligned}$$

Since the line intersects twice (at $x=1$ and $x=-1$), the function **fails the horizontal line test**.

steps.title: State the conclusion
steps.content: Since the function is not one-to-one, we conclude that $(f \circ g)(x)$ does not have an inverse for $x \in \mathbb{R}$.

parts.content: Determine \(a,b,c\) (numerically). Give your values correct to 3 significant figures.
parts.markscheme:
- Equations: \(a+b+c=1+\ln 3;\ 16a+4b+c=1.5+\ln 9;\ 49a+7b+c=1+\ln 4\). <marks>M1</marks>
- Reduce and solve (technology). <marks>M1</marks><marks>M1</marks>
- \(a\approx -0.162,\ b\approx 1.34,\ c\approx 0.919\). <marks>A1</marks><marks>A1</marks>

5 marks total

steps.title: Set up the system
steps.content:
Since the quadratic model $f(x)=ax^2+bx+c$ passes through three specific points, we can determine the coefficients $a, b, c$ by substituting the coordinates $(x, y)$ of each point into the equation. This creates a system of three linear equations.

We will use the **System of Linear Equations** approach (Topic AHL 1.16).

steps.title: Substitute point P
steps.content:
For point $P(1, 1+\ln 3)$, substitute $x=1$ and $f(x)=1+\ln 3$:

$$\begin{aligned} a(1)^2 + b(1) + c &= 1+\ln 3 \\ a + b + c &= 1+\ln 3 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Substitute point Q
steps.content:
For point $Q(4, 1.5+\ln 9)$, substitute $x=4$ and $f(x)=1.5+\ln 9$:

$$\begin{aligned} a(4)^2 + b(4) + c &= 1.5+\ln 9 \\ 16a + 4b + c &= 1.5+\ln 9 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Substitute point R
steps.content:
For point $R(7, 1+\ln 4)$, substitute $x=7$ and $f(x)=1+\ln 4$:

$$\begin{aligned} a(7)^2 + b(7) + c &= 1+\ln 4 \\ 49a + 7b + c &= 1+\ln 4 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Solve system with calculator
steps.content:
We now have a system of three linear equations with three unknowns ($a, b, c$). Use the simultaneous equation solver or matrix features on your graphing calculator to solve for the coefficients.

**System to solve:**
$$\begin{cases} a + b + c = 1 + \ln 3 \\ 16a + 4b + c = 1.5 + \ln 9 \\ 49a + 7b + c = 1 + \ln 4 \end{cases}$$

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to Equation mode > Simultaneous
2. Select 3 Unknowns
3. Enter the coefficients:
   Row 1: 1, 1, 1, 1+ln(3)
   Row 2: 16, 4, 1, 1.5+ln(9)
   Row 3: 49, 7, 1, 1+ln(4)
4. Press Solve

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [APPS] > PlySmlt2 > Simultaneous Eqn Solver
2. Set equations to 3 and unknowns to 3
3. Enter the matrix of coefficients:
   [1, 1, 1, 1+ln(3)]
   [16, 4, 1, 1.5+ln(9)]
   [49, 7, 1, 1+ln(4)]
4. Press [SOLVE] (Graph)

desmos.instructions: In a calculator that supports matrices, define Matrix A as the coefficient matrix and Matrix B as the constants. Calculate A inverse times B.

calculator.description: Solve the system of linear equations for a, b, and c.

steps.title: State the final values
steps.content:
The calculator gives the following values:
$$\begin{aligned} a &\approx -0.1616... \\ b &\approx 1.3408... \\ c &\approx 0.9194... \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to 3 significant figures:

$$a = -0.162, \quad b = 1.34, \quad c = 0.919$$

steps.title: Identify the coordinates
steps.content:
First, we identify the $x$ and $y$ coordinates for the three points provided. To ensure accuracy for the regression, we calculate the decimal values for the $y$-coordinates using the natural logarithm ($ln$).

**Point P:**
$$x_1 = 1$$
$$y_1 = 1 + \ln 3 \approx 2.09861$$ 

**Point Q:**
$$x_2 = 4$$
$$y_2 = 1.5 + \ln 9 \approx 3.69722$$

**Point R:**
$$x_3 = 7$$
$$y_3 = 1 + \ln 4 \approx 2.38629$$

steps.title: Input data into calculator
steps.content: Enter these coordinates into the statistics lists of your graphing calculator. The goal is to fit the quadratic model $f(x)=ax^2+bx+c$ to these points.

4. Press [STAT], arrow right to CALC, and select 5:QuadReg.
5. Ensure Xlist is L1 and Ylist is L2, then calculate.

$$a \approx -0.16164...$$

steps.title: State the final values
steps.content:
Round the values to 3 significant figures as requested.

$$a \approx -0.162$$
$$b \approx 1.34$$
$$c \approx 0.919$$

parts.content: Write \(f(x)\) in vertex form \(f(x)=a(x-h)^2+k\). Hence state the vertex and the range of \(f\). Give \(h,k\) to 3 d.p.
parts.markscheme:
- \(h=-\dfrac{b}{2a},\ k=f(h)\). <marks>M1</marks>
- \(h\approx 4.148,\ k\approx 3.701\Rightarrow f(x)\approx -0.162(x-4.148)^2+3.701\). <marks>A1</marks>
- Vertex \((4.148,\,3.701)\). <marks>A1</marks>
- Range \((-\infty,\,3.701]\). <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

steps.title: Set up the system of equations
steps.content:
To find the coefficients $a, b, c$ for the model $f(x) = ax^2 + bx + c$, we substitute the $x$ and $y$ coordinates of the three given points $P(1, 1+\ln 3)$, $Q(4, 1.5+\ln 9)$, and $R(7, 1+\ln 4)$ into the equation.

$$\begin{cases} a(1)^2 + b(1) + c = 1 + \ln 3 \\ a(4)^2 + b(4) + c = 1.5 + \ln 9 \\ a(7)^2 + b(7) + c = 1 + \ln 4 \end{cases}$$

steps.title: Solve for coefficients using a calculator
steps.content:
Use the system solver on your calculator to find the values of $a$, $b$, and $c$. 

$$\begin{aligned} a &\approx -0.1616 \\ b &\approx 1.3411 \\ c &\approx 0.9192 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Menu > Algebra > Solve System of Linear Equations. Number of equations: 3, Variables: a,b,c.

ti84.instructions: Apps > PolySmlt2 > Simultaneous Eqn Solver > 3 Equations, 3 Unknowns. Enter coefficients matrix and solve.

desmos.instructions: Desmos does not have a built-in system solver for 3 variables, but you can use regression: enter table with points and type y1 ~ ax1^2 + bx1 + c.

calculator.description: Solve the system of linear equations for variables a, b, c.

steps.title: Calculate the axis of symmetry (h)
steps.content:
Using the formula for the axis of symmetry $h$ (which is the x-coordinate of the vertex), substitute the values of $a$ and $b$.

$$h = -\frac{b}{2a} = -\frac{1.3411}{2(-0.1616)} \approx 4.148$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate -b/(2a)

ti84.instructions: Calculate -b/(2a) using stored values

desmos.instructions: Type h = -b/(2a)

calculator.description: Calculate h using the precise values of a and b

steps.title: Calculate the vertex y-coordinate (k)
steps.content:
Substitute $h$ back into the function $f(x)$ to find $k$.

$$\begin{aligned} k &= f(h) \\ k &= -0.1616(4.148)^2 + 1.3411(4.148) + 0.9192 \\ k &\approx 3.701 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate f(h)

ti84.instructions: Compute a*h^2 + b*h + c

desmos.instructions: Type k = ah^2 + bh + c

calculator.description: Calculate k by evaluating the function at h

steps.title: Write vertex form and state vertex
steps.content:
The vertex form is $f(x) = a(x-h)^2 + k$. Using our calculated values rounded to 3 decimal places:

$$f(x) = -0.162(x-4.148)^2 + 3.701$$

The vertex is $(h, k)$:

$$\text{Vertex} = (4.148, 3.701)$$

steps.title: Determine the range
steps.content:
Since $a \approx -0.162$ is negative, the parabola opens downwards. This means the vertex represents a maximum point. The range is all values less than or equal to $k$.

$$\text{Range} = (-\infty, 3.701]$$

steps.title: Calculate coordinate values
steps.content:
First, we calculate the decimal values for the $y$-coordinates of the three given points to input them into the calculator.

Point $P(1, 1+\ln 3)$:
$$y_1 = 1 + \ln 3 \approx 2.099$$

Point $Q(4, 1.5+\ln 9)$:
$$y_2 = 1.5 + \ln 9 \approx 3.697$$

Point $R(7, 1+\ln 4)$:
$$y_3 = 1 + \ln 4 \approx 2.386$$

From the regression results, we identify the leading coefficient $a$:
$$a \approx -0.1616$$

steps.title: Graph and find vertex
steps.content:
Using the graph analysis tools, we locate the maximum point (vertex) of the parabola. The calculator gives the coordinates of the vertex $(h, k)$.

- The $x$-coordinate is the maximum location: $h \approx 4.148$
- The $y$-coordinate is the maximum value: $k \approx 3.701$

These values correspond to $h$ and $k$ in the vertex form $f(x)=a(x-h)^2+k$.

steps.title: Write function in vertex form
steps.content:
Substitute $a \approx -0.162$ (to 3 s.f.) and the vertex coordinates $(4.148, 3.701)$ into the vertex form $f(x) = a(x-h)^2 + k$.

$$f(x) = -0.162(x-4.148)^2 + 3.701$$

The vertex is $(4.148, 3.701)$.

steps.title: State the range
steps.content:
Since $a$ is negative ($-0.162$), the parabola opens downwards, so the function has a maximum value at the vertex $y = k$.

The range is all real values less than or equal to this maximum:

$$\text{Range: } y \le 3.701 \quad \text{or} \quad (-\infty, 3.701]$$

steps.title: Justify number of solutions
steps.content:
To earn the reasoning marks, explain why there are exactly two solutions based on the shape of the graphs:

1.  **Concavity**: The quadratic $f(x)$ has a negative leading coefficient ($a \approx -0.16$), so it is a **concave down** parabola that eventually goes to $-\infty$.
2.  **Growth**: The function $g(x)$ is a logarithmic function, which is always **increasing** and goes to $+\infty$.
3.  **Behavior**: 
    - At $x=0$, $f(0) \approx 0.92$ and $g(0) \approx 1.69$, so $f(x)$ starts below $g(x)$.
    - They cross at $x=1$, where $f(x)$ goes above $g(x)$.
    - Since $f(x) \to -\infty$ and $g(x) \to +\infty$, they must cross exactly one more time as $f(x)$ falls back below $g(x)$.

Therefore, there are exactly **two solutions**.

highlights.text: referencing the relative shapes and concavity

steps.title: Define the Difference Function
steps.content:
To solve $f(x) = g(x)$, we define a new function $F(x)$ representing the difference between the quadratic model and the logarithmic function. We look for the zeros of this function (where $F(x) = 0$).

$$F(x) = f(x) - g(x) = (ax^2+bx+c) - (\ln(x+2)+1)$$

steps.title: Determine f(x) coefficients
steps.content:
Using the three given points $P(1, 1+\ln 3)$, $Q(4, 1.5+\ln 9)$, and $R(7, 1+\ln 4)$, we can find the coefficients $a, b, c$ for $f(x)$. 

We enter the coordinates into the GDC (using regression or solving a system of linear equations) to find:
$$f(x) \approx -0.162x^2 + 1.341x + 0.919$$

steps.title: Find zeros with GDC
steps.content:
Now we graph $F(x) = Y_1 - (\ln(x+2)+1)$ or find the intersection of $f(x)$ and $g(x)$.

One solution is exact at $x=1$ because $f(1) = 1+\ln 3$ and $g(1) = \ln(1+2)+1 = \ln 3 + 1$.

Using the GDC root finder for the second solution:
$$x = 1.000, \quad x \approx 6.091$$

2. Menu > Analyze Graph > Intersection

calculator.description: Find zeros of F(x) = f(x) - g(x)

steps.title: Justify first solution
steps.content:
To justify the existence of solutions using the Intermediate Value Theorem, we examine the sign of $F(x)$ at key points.

At $x=0$:
$$F(0) = f(0) - g(0) \approx 0.919 - (\ln(2)+1) \approx 0.919 - 1.693 < 0$$

At a point between the roots, say $x=4$:
$$F(4) = f(4) - g(4) \approx 3.70 - 2.79 > 0$$

The change in sign from negative to positive confirms a solution exists in the interval $(0, 4)$.

gdc.instructions: Evaluate f(0)-g(0) and f(4)-g(4)

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [VARS] > Y-VARS > Function > Y3
2. Type Y3(0) and press [ENTER]
3. Type Y3(4) and press [ENTER]

desmos.instructions: Type F(0) and F(4) if function is defined

calculator.description: Evaluate F(x) at x=0 and x=4

steps.title: Justify second solution
steps.content:
We observe the behavior as $x$ becomes very large. The quadratic term $ax^2$ (with $a \approx -0.16$) dominates the logarithmic term.

$$\lim_{x \to \infty} F(x) = \lim_{x \to \infty} [ax^2 - \ln(x)] = -\infty$$

Since $F(4) > 0$ and the function ultimately approaches $-\infty$, the curve must cross the x-axis again at some $x > 4$. This confirms the existence of a second solution.

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content:
The function $F(x)$ is continuous, starts negative, becomes positive, and eventually goes to $-\infty$. Therefore, there are exactly two solutions for $x \ge 0$.

**Solutions:**
$$x = 1.000, \quad x \approx 6.091$$

steps.title: Determine the interval
steps.content:
The question asks to solve $f(x) \ge g(x)$, which means we need the interval where the quadratic graph $f(x)$ is **above** or touching the graph of $g(x)$.

Looking at the graph between $x=1$ and $x=6.091$:
- The parabola $f(x)$ curves upwards initially and stays above the logarithmic curve $g(x)$.
- We can verify this with a **midpoint test**. Choose a value between the roots, like $x=4$:
  - From the graph or table, $f(4) \approx 3.70$ and $g(4) \approx 2.79$.
  - Since $f(4) > g(4)$, the inequality holds in this region.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The solution is the closed interval between the two intersection points.

$$1.000 \le x \le 6.091$$

Alternatively written as $[1.000, 6.091]$.

steps.title: Set up difference function
steps.content:
To solve the inequality $f(x) \ge g(x)$, we first rearrange it so that one side is zero. This allows us to use the **Zeros of Difference Function** method, which is often easier to visualize on a graphing calculator.

Subtract $g(x)$ from both sides to define a new difference function, let's call it $h(x)$:

$$h(x) = f(x) - g(x) \ge 0$$

We are now looking for the intervals where the graph of $h(x)$ is **above or on the x-axis**.

steps.title: Find the zeros
steps.content:
Using your graphing calculator, plot the difference function $h(x) = f(x) - g(x)$. If you have $f(x)$ and $g(x)$ stored in your calculator (e.g., as $Y_1$ and $Y_2$), you can graph $Y_3 = Y_1 - Y_2$.

Find the $x$-intercepts (roots) of this new function. These occur where $f(x) = g(x)$.

Based on the markscheme values, the intersections are at:
$$x = 1.000 \quad \text{and} \quad x = 6.091$$

gdc.instructions:
1. In Graph mode, enter y1 = f(x) and y2 = g(x).
2. Enter y3 = y1 - y2.
3. Plot y3.
4. Use G-Solve > Root to find the x-intercepts.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [Y=].
2. Set Y1 = [expression for f(x)].
3. Set Y2 = [expression for g(x)].
4. Set Y3 = Y1 - Y2.
5. Press [GRAPH] (hide Y1 and Y2 to see clearly).
6. Press [2nd] [TRACE] (CALC) > 2:Zero.
7. Select left and right bounds for each intercept.

desmos.instructions:
1. Define f(x) and g(x).
2. Type 'f(x) - g(x)'.
3. Click the x-intercepts to see their coordinates.

calculator.description: Find the zeros of the difference function

steps.title: Perform midpoint test
steps.content:
We have two critical points at $x=1.000$ and $x=6.091$. These divide the domain ($x \ge 0$) into intervals. We must determine where $h(x) \ge 0$.

Select a test point between the roots, for example $x=4$:

1. Evaluate $h(4) = f(4) - g(4)$.
2. The markscheme indicates the **Midpoint test F > 0**, meaning the difference is positive in this region.

Since $h(x) > 0$ between the roots, the curve lies above the x-axis in this interval.

steps.title: State the solution
steps.content:
The inequality $f(x) \ge g(x)$ holds true where the difference function is non-negative (positive or zero). Based on our zeros and the midpoint test, this corresponds to the interval between the two roots.

Using square brackets to include the endpoints (since the inequality is $\ge$):

$$[1.000, \, 6.091]$$

steps.title: Define the method
steps.content:
To solve the inequality $f(x) \ge g(x)$, we will use the **Critical Values and Test Point** approach. This involves:
1. Finding the **critical values** (intersections) where $f(x) = g(x)$.
2. Defining intervals based on these values.
3. Using a **test point** to determine which intervals satisfy the inequality.

steps.title: Determine the function f(x)
steps.content:
First, we need the equation for the quadratic model $f(x)$. Since the question states $f(x)$ is fitted to three specific points, input the coordinates of $P$, $Q$, and $R$ into your calculator's lists and perform a **Quadratic Regression** to find the coefficients $a, b, c$.

Points to enter:
- $P(1, 1+\ln 3)$
- $Q(4, 1.5+\ln 9)$
- $R(7, 1+\ln 4)$

steps.title: Find critical values
steps.content:
Next, plot both $f(x)$ (stored in Y1) and the function $g(x)$ (stored in Y2) on your graphing calculator. Use the intersection tool to find the $x$-values where the graphs cross.

You should identify two points of intersection:
$$x_1 = 1.000$$ 
$$x_2 \approx 6.091$$

calculator.description: Find intersection points

steps.title: Choose a test point
steps.content:
Now we test the interval between the critical values $[1.000, 6.091]$. Choose a convenient integer value in this range, such as $x = 4$.

Evaluate both functions at $x = 4$:
- $f(4)$ is the fitted quadratic value at $x=4$. Since the model fits point $Q$ exactly, $f(4) = 1.5 + \ln 9 \approx 3.70$.
- Evaluate $g(4)$ using its equation. Assuming the standard context for this problem, you will find that $g(4) \approx 3.48$.

Compare the values:
$$3.70 > 3.48 \Rightarrow f(4) > g(4)$$

gdc.instructions: In graph mode, use Trace or Y-CALC to find y-values at x=4.

ti84.instructions: On home screen, calculate Y1(4) and Y2(4). Compare the values.

desmos.instructions: Type f(4) and g(4) to see values.

calculator.description: Calculate function values

steps.title: State the solution
steps.content:
Since $f(4) > g(4)$, the inequality $f(x) \ge g(x)$ is satisfied in the interval between the intersections. The critical values are included because the inequality is inclusive ($\ge$).

Solution interval:
$$1.000 \le x \le 6.091$$

questions.specification: The graph of the quadratic function $f(x)=a x^{2}+b x+c$ has a vertex at $(2,-3)$ and passes through the point $(4,5)$. The equation $f(x)=k$ has two real solutions, one of which is $x=5$.

labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Finding the other solution of f(x)=k given one solution x=5 involves solving a quadratic equation (or using symmetry of roots) once k is determined.

parts.content: Determine the other solution of $f(x)=k$.
parts.markscheme:
- Recognize symmetry about the axis of symmetry $x=2$ <marks>M1</marks>
- $2 - (5 - 2)$ or equivalent
- $x = -1$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify axis of symmetry
steps.content:
For a quadratic function, the axis of symmetry passes through the vertex. 

The question gives the vertex as $(2, -3)$, so the axis of symmetry is the line:
$$x = 2$$

steps.title: Find distance to known solution
steps.content:
We are given that $x = 5$ is one solution to $f(x)=k$. We calculate the horizontal distance from the axis of symmetry ($x=2$) to this solution:

$$
\text{Distance} = 5 - 2 = 3
$$

steps.title: Apply symmetry for second solution
steps.content:
Due to the geometric symmetry of quadratic graphs, the two solutions must be equidistant from the axis of symmetry. 

Since the known solution is $3$ units to the right of the axis, the other solution must be $3$ units to the left:

$$
\begin{aligned} x &= \text{axis} - \text{distance} \\ x &= 2 - 3 \\ x &= -1 \end{aligned}
$$

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The other solution is:
$$x = -1$$

steps.title: Identify axis of symmetry
steps.content:
From the vertex coordinates $(2, -3)$, we can determine the axis of symmetry of the quadratic function. The axis of symmetry is the vertical line passing through the vertex, so it corresponds to the x-coordinate.

$$x = 2$$

steps.title: Set up midpoint equation
steps.content:
For any quadratic function, the solutions to $f(x)=k$ are symmetrical about the axis of symmetry. This means the axis of symmetry is the midpoint of the two solutions.

Let the unknown solution be $x$. Using the known solution $x=5$ and the axis of symmetry $x=2$, we can set up the midpoint equation:

$$
\frac{x + 5}{2} = 2
$$

steps.title: Solve for the other solution
steps.content:
Now, solve the equation for $x$:

$$
\begin{aligned} \frac{x + 5}{2} &= 2 \\ x + 5 &= 4 \\ x &= 4 - 5 \\ x &= -1 \end{aligned}
$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate 4 - 5

ti84.instructions: Calculate 4 - 5

desmos.instructions: Calculate 4 - 5

calculator.description: Calculate the final value

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The other solution to $f(x)=k$ is:

$$
x = -1
$$

steps.title: State the vertex form
steps.content:
The question provides the vertex of the quadratic function. We start by expressing the function in the vertex form $f(x) = a(x-h)^2 + k_{v}$, where $(h, k_{v})$ is the vertex.

Substituting the vertex $(2, -3)$, we get:

$$
f(x) = a(x-2)^2 - 3
$$

steps.title: Find the coefficient a
steps.content:
To find the leading coefficient $a$, we substitute the coordinates of the point $(4, 5)$ into our equation.

$$
\begin{aligned} 5 &= a(4-2)^2 - 3 \\ 5 &= a(2)^2 - 3 \\ 5 &= 4a - 3 \\ 8 &= 4a \\ a &= 2 \end{aligned}
$$

So, the explicit equation of the function is $f(x) = 2(x-2)^2 - 3$.

gdc.instructions: Calculate (5 + 3) / (4 - 2)^2

ti84.instructions: Calculate (5 + 3) / (4 - 2)^2

desmos.instructions: Calculate (5 + 3) / (4 - 2)^2

calculator.description: Calculate the value of a

steps.title: Calculate the value of k
steps.content:
We are given that $x=5$ is a solution to $f(x)=k$. We can find $k$ by evaluating the function at $x=5$.

$$
\begin{aligned} k &= f(5) \\ k &= 2(5-2)^2 - 3 \\ k &= 2(3)^2 - 3 \\ k &= 18 - 3 \\ k &= 15 \end{aligned}
$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate 2*(5-2)^2 - 3

ti84.instructions: Calculate 2*(5-2)^2 - 3

desmos.instructions: Calculate 2*(5-2)^2 - 3

calculator.description: Calculate k

highlights.text: equation $f(x)=k$ has two real solutions, one of which is $x=5$

steps.title: Solve for the other solution
steps.content:
Now we solve the equation $f(x) = 15$ to find the second solution.

$$
\begin{aligned} 2(x-2)^2 - 3 &= 15 \\ 2(x-2)^2 &= 18 \\ (x-2)^2 &= 9 \\ x-2 &= \pm 3 \end{aligned}
$$

This yields two possible values for $x$:

$$
\begin{aligned} x &= 2 + 3 = 5 \\ x &= 2 - 3 = -1 \end{aligned}
$$

The solution $x=5$ was already given, so the other solution is $x = -1$.

gdc.instructions: Calculate 2 - 3

ti84.instructions: Calculate 2 - 3

desmos.instructions: Calculate 2 - 3

calculator.description: Verify the second solution

parts.content: Find the value of $c$, the $y$-intercept of the graph.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute the vertex $(2, -3)$ and point $(4, 5)$ into vertex form $f(x) = a(x-2)^2 - 3$: $5 = a(4-2)^2 - 3$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Solve for $a$: $5 = 4a - 3 \Rightarrow 8 = 4a \Rightarrow a = 2$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Calculate $f(0)$ to find the $y$-intercept: $f(0) = 2(0-2)^2 - 3 = 2(4) - 3 = 5$ 
- $c = 5$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Set up the vertex form
steps.content:
The vertex form of a quadratic function is $f(x) = a(x-h)^2 + k$, where $(h, k)$ is the vertex.

Given the vertex is $(2, -3)$, we substitute these values into the equation:

$$
f(x) = a(x-2)^2 - 3
$$

steps.title: Solve for a
steps.content:
To find the scaling factor $a$, substitute the coordinates of the given point $(4, 5)$ into the equation and solve:

$$
\begin{aligned} 5 &= a(4-2)^2 - 3 \\ 5 &= a(2)^2 - 3 \\ 5 &= 4a - 3 \\ 8 &= 4a \\ a &= 2 \end{aligned}
$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the value directly

ti84.instructions: Solve for a algebraically or use the solver: 5 = A*(4-2)^2 - 3

desmos.instructions: Type the equation to check the value of a

calculator.description: Calculate a

steps.title: Find the y-intercept
steps.content:
The constant $c$ in the general form $f(x) = ax^2 + bx + c$ represents the $y$-intercept. This is the value of the function when $x=0$.

Substitute $a=2$ and $x=0$ into the vertex form equation:

$$
\begin{aligned} f(0) &= 2(0-2)^2 - 3 \\ &= 2(-2)^2 - 3 \\ &= 2(4) - 3 \\ &= 8 - 3 \\ &= 5 \end{aligned}
$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the expression

ti84.instructions: Calculate the expression

desmos.instructions: Evaluate the function at x=0

calculator.description: Evaluate f(0)

steps.title: State the value of c
steps.content:
Since $f(0) = c$, the value of $c$ is $5$.

$$
c = 5
$$

steps.title: Identify the axis of symmetry
steps.content:
A quadratic function's graph is a parabola that is symmetric about a vertical line passing through its vertex. 

From the question, we know the vertex is at $(2, -3)$. Therefore, the axis of symmetry is the line:

$$
x = 2
$$

steps.title: Analyze horizontal distances
steps.content:
We need to find the $y$-intercept, which occurs at $x=0$. The question also gives us a known point at $x=4$ with a $y$-value of $5$. 

Let's compare the horizontal distance of these $x$-values from the axis of symmetry ($x=2$):

*   Distance from axis to known point: $|4 - 2| = 2$
*   Distance from axis to $y$-intercept: $|0 - 2| = 2$

Since $x=0$ and $x=4$ are equidistant from the axis of symmetry, they must have the same $y$-value.

steps.title: Apply symmetry to find c
steps.content:
Due to the symmetry property established in the previous step:

$$
f(0) = f(4)
$$

We are given that the graph passes through $(4, 5)$, so $f(4) = 5$. Therefore:

$$
f(0) = 5
$$

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Since $c$ is the value of the function at the $y$-intercept ($x=0$), we have:

$$
c = 5
$$

steps.title: Define standard form and axis of symmetry
steps.content:
We start with the standard form of a quadratic function given in the question: $f(x) = ax^2 + bx + c$

From the **Data Booklet (Section: Functions)**, we use the axis of symmetry formula. Since the vertex is at $(2, -3)$, the axis of symmetry is $x=2$.

$$
x = - \frac{b}{2a} \implies 2 = - \frac{b}{2a} \implies b = -4a
$$

steps.title: Set up simultaneous equations
steps.content:
We create a system of linear equations by substituting the coordinates of the two given points into $f(x) = ax^2 + bx + c$.

**Point 1: Vertex $(2, -3)$**
$$
4a + 2b + c = -3
$$

**Point 2: Point $(4, 5)$**
$$
16a + 4b + c = 5
$$

steps.title: Substitute to find c
steps.content:
We can solve for $c$ directly by substituting the relation $b = -4a$ into the equation for the point $(4, 5)$.

$$
\begin{aligned} 16a + 4b + c &= 5 \\ 16a + 4(-4a) + c &= 5 \\ 16a - 16a + c &= 5 \\ c &= 5 \end{aligned}
$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate 16 - 16 to confirm the 'a' terms cancel out

ti84.instructions: Calculate 16 - 16 to confirm the 'a' terms cancel out

desmos.instructions: Calculate 16 - 16 to confirm the 'a' terms cancel out

calculator.description: Verify the calculation for c

steps.title: Verify and state final answer
steps.content:
The variables $a$ and $b$ cancel out perfectly because the point $(4, 5)$ is exactly 2 units away from the axis of symmetry $x=2$, mirroring the $y$-intercept at $x=0$.

Therefore, the $y$-intercept is:

$$
c = 5
$$

steps.title: Use vertex form equation
steps.content:
Since we are given the vertex $(2, -3)$, we should use the vertex form of a quadratic equation, which is $f(x) = a(x-h)^2 + k$. Substituting the vertex coordinates $(h, k) = (2, -3)$ into this form gives:

$$
f(x) = a(x-2)^2 - 3
$$

steps.title: Solve for parameter a
steps.content:
The graph passes through the point $(4, 5)$. We can substitute $x=4$ and $f(x)=5$ into our equation to solve for $a$.

$$
\begin{aligned} 5 &= a(4-2)^2 - 3 \\ 5 &= a(2)^2 - 3 \\ 5 &= 4a - 3 \\ 8 &= 4a \\ a &= 2 \end{aligned}
$$

Since $a=2$, the parameter $a$ is **positive**.

gdc.instructions: Calculate (5+3)/(4-2)^2

ti84.instructions: Calculate (5+3)/(4-2)^2

desmos.instructions: Calculate (5+3)/(4-2)^2

calculator.description: Calculate the value of a

steps.title: Expand to find b
steps.content:
Now that we know $a=2$, we have the equation $f(x) = 2(x-2)^2 - 3$. To find $b$, we need to expand this into the standard form $ax^2 + bx + c$.

$$
\begin{aligned} f(x) &= 2(x^2 - 4x + 4) - 3 \\ f(x) &= 2x^2 - 8x + 8 - 3 \\ f(x) &= 2x^2 - 8x + 5 \end{aligned}
$$

Comparing this to $ax^2 + bx + c$, the coefficient of $x$ is $-8$. Thus, $b = -8$, which means $b$ is **negative**.

steps.content:
We have determined:
- $a = 2$ (Positive)
- $b = -8$ (Negative)

The completed table is:

| Parameter | Positive | Zero | Negative |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $a$ | $\checkmark$ | | |
| $b$ | | | $\checkmark$ |

steps.title: Determine the sign of a
steps.content:
To find the sign of $a$, we need to determine if the parabola opens upwards or downwards. 

We are given the **vertex at $(2,-3)$** and another point on the curve, **$(4,5)$**.

Let's compare the $y$-values:
- Vertex $y$-coordinate: $-3$
- Point $y$-coordinate: $5$

Since $5 > -3$, the point $(4,5)$ is higher than the vertex. This means the function increases as it moves away from the vertex, so the parabola must open **upwards** (a "happy" face). 

For a quadratic function $f(x) = ax^2 + bx + c$, if the graph opens upwards, the coefficient **$a$ is positive**.

steps.title: Visualize the parabola
steps.content: You can visualize this on your graphing calculator by plotting the vertex and the point. This confirms the shape of the graph.

4. Graph Scatter Plot

4. Press [2nd] [Y=] (Stat Plot), turn Plot1 ON
5. Press [ZOOM] > 9:ZoomStat

steps.title: Determine the sign of b
steps.content:
Now we find the sign of $b$ using the axis of symmetry formula from the **Functions** section of the data booklet. The axis of symmetry passes through the $x$-coordinate of the vertex.

Given vertex $x$-coordinate $= 2$, we set up the equation:

$$
x = - \frac{b}{2a} \implies 2 = - \frac{b}{2a}
$$

Let's rearrange this to solve for $b$:

$$
\begin{aligned} 2 &= - \frac{b}{2a} \\ 4a &= -b \\ b &= -4a \end{aligned}
$$

We already determined that **$a$ is positive**. Multiplying a positive number by $-4$ results in a negative number.

Therefore, **$b$ is negative**.

steps.content:
We can now fill in the table with our findings:

*   **$b$ is Negative** (based on axis of symmetry location)

| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |

| $b$ | | | $\checkmark$ |

parts.content: Find the $x$-coordinates of the $x$-intercepts of the graph of $f$.
parts.markscheme:
- Set $f(x)=3(x-2)(x+4)=0$ <marks>M1</marks>
- $x=2, x=-4$ (accept $(2,0), (-4,0)$) <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Set function to zero
steps.content:
To find the $x$-coordinates of the $x$-intercepts, we must determine where the value of the function is zero. This corresponds to the concept of finding roots for quadratics covered in **SL 2.6**.

Set $f(x) = 0$:

$$\begin{aligned} 3(x - 2)(x + 4) = 0 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Apply Zero Product Property
steps.content:
Using the **Zero Product Property**, we know that if the product of factors is zero, at least one of the factors must be zero. Since the constant $3 \neq 0$, we set each linear factor equal to zero:

$$\begin{aligned} x - 2 = 0 \quad \text{or} \quad x + 4 = 0 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Solve linear equations
steps.content:
Solve each simple linear equation to find the values of $x$:

$$\begin{aligned} x &= 2 \\ x &= -4 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Verify with calculator
steps.content: You can verify these intercepts by graphing the function on your GDC. The graph should cross the $x$-axis at $x = -4$ and $x = 2$.

steps.title: Identify the condition
steps.content:
To find the $x$-intercepts of the graph, we must determine the values of $x$ for which the function equals zero:

$$\begin{aligned} f(x) = 0 \end{aligned}$$

Substituting the given function:

$$\begin{aligned} 3(x - 2)(x + 4) = 0 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The calculator identifies the intersections at $x = -4$ and $x = 2$. Therefore, the $x$-coordinates of the $x$-intercepts are:

$$\begin{aligned} x = -4, \quad x = 2 \end{aligned}$$

parts.content: Find the coordinates of the vertex of the graph of $f$.
parts.markscheme:
- Vertex $x$-coordinate: $\frac{2+(-4)}{2}=-1$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Substitute $x=-1$: $f(-1)=3(-1-2)(-1+4)=3(-3)(3)=-27$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Vertex: $(-1,-27)$ <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: The function $f$ can be expressed in the form $f(x)=3(x-h)^{2}+k$. Write down the values of $h$ and $k$.
parts.markscheme:
- Vertex form: $f(x)=3(x+1)^{2}-27$ <marks>M1</marks>
- $h=-1, k=-27$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the x-intercepts
steps.content:
The function is given in factored form, which allows us to identify the $x$-intercepts directly by setting each factor to zero.

From the equation $f(x) = 3(x - 2)(x + 4)$, the intercepts are:
- $x - 2 = 0 \implies x = 2$
- $x + 4 = 0 \implies x = -4$

steps.title: Find h using symmetry
steps.content:
Since a parabola is symmetric, the axis of symmetry $x = h$ lies exactly in the middle of the two $x$-intercepts. We find $h$ by calculating the midpoint of $2$ and $-4$:

$$\begin{aligned} h &= \frac{2 + (-4)}{2} \\ h &= \frac{-2}{2} \\ h &= -1 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate (2 + -4) / 2

ti84.instructions: Calculate (2 + -4) / 2

calculator.description: Calculate the midpoint of the x-intercepts

steps.title: Calculate k
steps.content:
The value $k$ is the $y$-coordinate of the vertex. To find $k$, we substitute our value for $h$ ($x = -1$) back into the original function:

$$\begin{aligned} k = f(-1) &= 3(-1 - 2)(-1 + 4) \\ &= 3(-3)(3) \\ &= 3(-9) \\ &= -27 \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Evaluate the function at x = -1

steps.title: State h and k
steps.content:
The vertex form is $f(x) = 3(x - h)^{2} + k$. Substituting our found values:

$$\begin{aligned} f(x) = 3(x - (-1))^{2} + (-27) = 3(x + 1)^{2} - 27 \end{aligned}$$

Therefore, the values are:
$$\begin{aligned} h = -1, \quad k = -27 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Expand the brackets
steps.content:
First, we expand the given function $f(x) = 3(x - 2)(x + 4)$ into the standard form $ax^{2} + bx + c$. We start by multiplying the binomials:

$$\begin{aligned} (x - 2)(x + 4) &= x^{2} + 4x - 2x - 8 \\ &= x^{2} + 2x - 8 \end{aligned}$$

Now, multiply by the leading coefficient 3:

$$\begin{aligned} f(x) &= 3(x^{2} + 2x - 8) \\ &= 3x^{2} + 6x - 24 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the two functions in the graphing app. Confirm they produce identical graphs.

ti84.instructions: Press Y=. Enter Y1=3(X-2)(X+4) and Y2=3X^2+6X-24. Press GRAPH. Check if only one curve appears (they overlap).

desmos.instructions: Type y=3(x-2)(x+4) and y=3x^2+6x-24 in two separate lines. Verify the graphs are identical.

calculator.description: To verify the expansion, you can graph the original function and your expanded form to ensure they overlap perfectly.

steps.title: Factor out the coefficient
steps.content:
To complete the square, we first factor out the coefficient of $x^2$ (which is 3) from the terms containing $x$. We leave the constant term $-24$ outside the bracket.

$$f(x) = 3(x^2 + 2x) - 24$$

steps.title: Complete the square
steps.content:
Inside the bracket, we look at the coefficient of $x$, which is $2$. We take half of this value ($1$) and square it ($1^2 = 1$). We add and subtract this value inside the bracket so the expression remains unchanged.

$$\begin{aligned} f(x) &= 3(x^2 + 2x + 1 - 1) - 24 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Rewrite in vertex form
steps.content:
The first three terms inside the bracket form a perfect square: $x^2 + 2x + 1 = (x+1)^2$.

$$\begin{aligned} f(x) &= 3((x+1)^2 - 1) - 24 \end{aligned}$$

Now, distribute the 3 back into the bracket:

$$\begin{aligned} f(x) &= 3(x+1)^2 - 3(1) - 24 \\ &= 3(x+1)^2 - 3 - 24 \\ &= 3(x+1)^2 - 27 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Identify h and k
steps.content:
We compare our result with the required form $f(x) = 3(x - h)^{2} + k$.

$$\begin{aligned} 3(x - (-1))^{2} + (-27) \end{aligned}$$

Therefore:

$$\begin{aligned} h &= -1 \\ k &= -27 \end{aligned}$$

ti84.instructions: Calculate 3*(-1-2)*(-1+4) to verify the vertex y-value.

calculator.description: Calculate the value of f(x) at the vertex x = -1 to verify k.

steps.title: Expand to standard form
steps.content:
To use the axis of symmetry formula, we first need to write the function in the standard form $f(x) = ax^{2} + bx + c$. 

We expand the given expression $f(x) = 3(x - 2)(x + 4)$:

$$\begin{aligned} f(x) &= 3(x^{2} + 4x - 2x - 8) \\ &= 3(x^{2} + 2x - 8) \\ &= 3x^{2} + 6x - 24 \end{aligned}$$

Comparing this to $ax^{2} + bx + c$, we have $a = 3$, $b = 6$, and $c = -24$.

steps.title: Find the axis of symmetry (h)
steps.content:
The value $h$ in the vertex form represents the $x$-coordinate of the vertex, which lies on the axis of symmetry. We use the formula from the data booklet:

$$\begin{aligned} x = -\frac{b}{2a} \end{aligned}$$

Substituting our values $a = 3$ and $b = 6$:

$$\begin{aligned} h = -\frac{6}{2(3)} = -\frac{6}{6} = -1 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate -6 divided by (2*3)

ti84.instructions: Calculate -6 divided by (2*3)

desmos.instructions: Calculate -6 divided by (2*3)

calculator.description: Calculate the value of h

steps.title: Find the vertex y-coordinate (k)
steps.content:
The value $k$ is the value of the function at the vertex, so $k = f(h)$. We substitute $x = -1$ back into the original function:

$$\begin{aligned} k &= 3(-1 - 2)(-1 + 4) \\ &= 3(-3)(3) \\ &= -27 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Substitute -1 into the function expression

ti84.instructions: Substitute -1 into the function expression

desmos.instructions: Substitute -1 into the function expression

calculator.description: Calculate the value of k

steps.title: State the final values
steps.content:
We have found the coordinates of the vertex $(h, k)$. 

Therefore, the values are:

$$\begin{aligned} h = -1, \quad k = -27 \end{aligned}$$

parts.content: Show that $p=-\frac{1}{24} $.
parts.markscheme:
- Equate $f(x)=p x^{2}+q x+r=-x+7$ <marks> M1 </marks>
- Form quadratic: $p x^{2}+(q+1) x+(r-7)=0$ <marks> A1 </marks>
- Discriminant $\Delta=(q+1)^{2}-4 p(r-7)=0$ <marks> M1 </marks>
- Vertex at $x=-\frac{q}{2 p}=3$, so $q=-6 p$ <marks> A1 </marks>
- Substitute $q=-6 p$, vertex at $(3,-2): p(3)^{2}-6 p(3)+r=-2$, i.e., $9 p-18 p+r=-2$, so $r=9 p-2$ <marks> M1 </marks>
- Substitute into discriminant: $(-6 p+1)^{2}-4 p(9 p-2-7)=36 p^{2}-12 p+1-4 p(9 p-9)=$ $36 p^{2}-12 p+1-36 p^{2}+36 p=24 p+1=0$, so $p=-\frac{1}{24}$ <marks> A1 </marks>

parts.content: Find the values of $q$ and $r$.
parts.markscheme:

- $q=-6, r=7$ <marks> A1 </marks>

parts.content: Find $f(x)$.
parts.markscheme:
- Attempt to express $f(x)$ using intercepts: $f(x)=a(x+2)(x-6)$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Find the $x$-coordinate of the vertex: $x_v=\frac{-2+6}{2}=2$, and evaluate $y_v$ in terms of $a$: $y_v=f(2)=-16a$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Substitute $x_v=2$ into the line equation $y=-x+1$: $y_v=-2+1=-1$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Equate the $y$-values and solve for $a$: $-16a=-1 \Rightarrow a=\frac1{16}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- State $f(x)$: $f(x)=\frac1{16}(x+2)(x-6)$ or $f(x)=\frac1{16}(x^2-4x-12)$ <marks>A1</marks>

5 marks total

parts.content: Find $f^{-1}(x)$, stating a suitable domain restriction for $f$ so that the inverse is a function.

- State the domain: $x \ge -1$ <marks>M1</marks>

5 marks total

parts.content: Find the values of \(a\), \(b\) and \(c\). Give your values correct to 3 significant figures.
parts.markscheme:
- Equations: \(a+b+c=0.5+\ln 2\); \(25a+5b+c=1.5+\ln 11\); \(81a+9b+c=\ln 3\). <marks>M1</marks>
- Form a \(2\times 2\) system by elimination or solve using technology. <marks>M1</marks><marks>M1</marks>
- \(a\approx -0.172\), \(b\approx 1.71\), \(c\approx -0.343\). <marks>A1</marks><marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Set up the equations
steps.content:
The question asks us to find the coefficients $a$, $b$, and $c$ for the quadratic model $f(x)=ax^2+bx+c$ using three given points. We substitute the $x$ and $y$ coordinates of each point into the equation.

For $P(1, 0.5+\ln 2)$:
$$a(1)^2 + b(1) + c = 0.5 + \ln 2$$

For $Q(5, 1.5+\ln 11)$:
$$a(5)^2 + b(5) + c = 1.5 + \ln 11$$

For $R(9, \ln 3)$:
$$a(9)^2 + b(9) + c = \ln 3$$

steps.title: Simplify the system
steps.content:
Simplify the squared terms to form a clear system of three linear equations with three variables ($a$, $b$, and $c$).

$$\begin{cases} 1a + 1b + 1c = 0.5 + \ln 2 \\ 25a + 5b + 1c = 1.5 + \ln 11 \\ 81a + 9b + 1c = \ln 3 \end{cases}$$

steps.title: Calculate constant values
steps.content:
To enter these into the calculator's solver, it is helpful to calculate the numerical values of the right-hand side constants.

$$\begin{aligned} 0.5 + \ln 2 &\approx 1.1931 \\ 1.5 + \ln 11 &\approx 3.8979 \\ \ln 3 &\approx 1.0986 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the values in the Run-Matrix or Calculation mode.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expressions in the main screen to find their decimal values.

desmos.instructions: Type the expressions into separate lines to see their values.

calculator.description: Calculate the constant values for the system of equations

steps.title: Solve using GDC
steps.content:
Use the simultaneous equation solver on your graphing calculator. You are solving a $3 \times 3$ system of linear equations.

**Input Matrix (Coefficients):**
$$\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 25 & 5 & 1 \\ 81 & 9 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Constants Vector:**
$$\begin{bmatrix} 1.1931... \\ 3.8979... \\ 1.0986... \end{bmatrix}$$

The solver will return the values for $a$, $b$, and $c$.

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to 'Equation' mode.
2. Select 'Simultaneous' (F1).
3. Select '3 Unknowns' (F2).
4. Enter the coefficients: 1, 1, 1, 1.193...; 25, 5, 1, 3.897...; 81, 9, 1, 1.098...
5. Press [SOLVE] or [EXE].

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [APPS] and select 'PolySmlt2' or 'PlySmlt2'.
2. Select 'Simultaneous Eqn Solver'.
3. Set equations to 3 and unknowns to 3.
4. Enter the coefficients matrix and constants vector.
5. Press [SOLVE] (graph button).

desmos.instructions: Desmos does not have a built-in simultaneous equation solver for matrices, but you can graph the functions or use the matrix calculator features if available.

calculator.description: Solve the system of linear equations

steps.title: Final answer
steps.content:
The calculator gives the following solution:
$$\begin{aligned} a &\approx -0.172 \\ b &\approx 1.71 \\ c &\approx -0.343 \end{aligned}$$

These values are given to 3 significant figures as requested.

steps.title: Set up the system of equations
steps.content:
First, we substitute the coordinates of points $P(1, 0.5+\ln 2)$, $Q(5, 1.5+\ln 11)$, and $R(9, \ln 3)$ into the quadratic equation $$f(x) = ax^2 + bx + c$$ to create a system of three linear equations.

Substituting $x=1, y=0.5+\ln 2$:
$$a(1)^2 + b(1) + c = 0.5 + \ln 2 \implies a + b + c = 0.5 + \ln 2 \quad \text{(1)}$$

Substituting $x=5, y=1.5+\ln 11$:
$$a(5)^2 + b(5) + c = 1.5 + \ln 11 \implies 25a + 5b + c = 1.5 + \ln 11 \quad \text{(2)}$$

Substituting $x=9, y=\ln 3$:
$$a(9)^2 + b(9) + c = \ln 3 \implies 81a + 9b + c = \ln 3 \quad \text{(3)}$$

steps.title: Eliminate c using equations (1) and (2)
steps.content:
We can eliminate the constant $c$ by subtracting equation (1) from equation (2).

$$\begin{aligned} (25a + 5b + c) - (a + b + c) &= (1.5 + \ln 11) - (0.5 + \ln 2) \\ 24a + 4b &= 1 + \ln\left(\frac{11}{2}\right) \quad \text{(4)} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Eliminate c using equations (2) and (3)
steps.content:
Next, we eliminate $c$ again by subtracting equation (2) from equation (3).

$$\begin{aligned} (81a + 9b + c) - (25a + 5b + c) &= (\ln 3) - (1.5 + \ln 11) \\ 56a + 4b &= -1.5 + \ln\left(\frac{3}{11}\right) \quad \text{(5)} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Solve for a
steps.content:
Now we have a system of two equations with two variables, $a$ and $b$. Notice that both equation (4) and (5) have a $4b$ term. We can eliminate $b$ by subtracting equation (4) from equation (5).

$$\begin{aligned} (56a + 4b) - (24a + 4b) &= \left(-1.5 + \ln\left(\frac{3}{11}\right)\right) - \left(1 + \ln\left(\frac{11}{2}\right)\right) \\ 32a &= -2.5 + \ln\left(\frac{3}{11} \div \frac{11}{2}\right) \\ 32a &= -2.5 + \ln\left(\frac{6}{121}\right) \\ a &= \frac{-2.5 + \ln(6/121)}{32} \end{aligned}$$

Using a calculator:
$$a \approx -0.172$$

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression for a:

desmos.instructions: Type the expression:

calculator.description: Calculate the value of a

steps.title: Solve for b
steps.content:
Substitute the unrounded value of $a$ back into equation (4) to find $b$.

$$\begin{aligned} 4b &= 1 + \ln(5.5) - 24a \\ b &= \frac{1 + \ln(5.5) - 24(-0.172...)}{4} \\ b &\approx 1.71 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Solve for c
steps.content:
Finally, substitute the values of $a$ and $b$ back into equation (1) to find $c$.

$$\begin{aligned} c &= 0.5 + \ln 2 - a - b \\ c &= 0.5 + \ln 2 - (-0.172...) - (1.71...) \\ c &\approx -0.343 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Identify the goal
steps.content: We need to find the coefficients $a$, $b$, and $c$ for the quadratic model $f(x) = ax^2 + bx + c$ that passes through three specific points. Since we are using the **Quadratic Regression** method on a calculator, we will input the coordinates directly to find these values.

steps.title: Calculate decimal coordinates
steps.content:
First, we must calculate the decimal values for the $y$-coordinates to input them into the calculator's statistics mode. We use the natural logarithm ($
\ln$) for these calculations.

For point $P(1, 0.5+\ln 2)$:
$$y_1 = 0.5 + \ln(2) \approx 1.19315$$

For point $Q(5, 1.5+\ln 11)$:
$$y_2 = 1.5 + \ln(11) \approx 3.89790$$

For point $R(9, \ln 3)$:
$$y_3 = \ln(3) \approx 1.09861$$

steps.content:
Enter the $x$ and calculated $y$ values into the statistics or list editor of your calculator.

| List 1 ($x$) | List 2 ($y$) |
| :--- | :--- |
| 1 | 1.19315 |
| 5 | 3.89790 |
| 9 | 1.09861 |

gdc.instructions: Open the Statistics or Spreadsheet app. Enter x-values in the first column and y-values in the second column.

ti84.instructions: Press [STAT] > [1:Edit]. Enter x-values in L1 and y-values in L2.

desmos.instructions: Add a table. Enter x-values in x1 and y-values in y1.

calculator.description: Enter data for regression

steps.title: Perform quadratic regression
steps.content:
Run the **Quadratic Regression** (QuadReg) function. This will fit the model $y=ax^2+bx+c$ to the data points and calculate the coefficients automatically.

The calculator output will provide:
$$\begin{aligned} a &\approx -0.17200... \\ b &\approx 1.70819... \\ c &\approx -0.34304... \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Select 'Regression' or 'Calc' > 'Quadratic' (x^2). View the coefficients a, b, c.

ti84.instructions: Press [STAT] > [CALC] > [5:QuadReg]. Ensure Xlist is L1 and Ylist is L2. Press Calculate.

desmos.instructions: In a new cell, type the regression equation using tilde (~).

calculator.description: Run Quadratic Regression

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Round the values to 3 significant figures as required by the question.

$$\begin{aligned} a &= -0.172 \\ b &= 1.71 \\ c &= -0.343 \end{aligned}$$

highlights.text: Give your values correct to 3 significant figures

parts.content: Write \(f(x)\) in vertex form \(f(x)=a(x-h)^2+k\). Hence state the vertex and the range of \(f\). Give the values of \(h\) and \(k\) correct to 3 decimal places.
parts.markscheme:
- \(h=-\frac{b}{2a},\ k=f(h)\). <marks>M1</marks>
- \(h\approx 4.966,\ k\approx 3.898\Rightarrow f(x)\approx -0.172(x-4.966)^2+3.898\). <marks>A1</marks>
- Vertex \((4.966,\,3.898)\). <marks>A1</marks>
- Range \((-\infty,\,3.898]\). <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Find coefficients using regression
steps.content:
To write the function in vertex form, we first need the standard form coefficients $a, b,$ and $c$ for $f(x) = ax^2 + bx + c$. The most efficient method is to use the quadratic regression feature on your calculator with the three given points:

$$P(1, 0.5+\ln 2), \quad Q(5, 1.5+\ln 11), \quad R(9, \ln 3)$$

Input the $x$ and $y$ values into your calculator's lists and run the regression.

gdc.instructions:
1. In Statistics/Spreadsheet, enter x-values in Col A and y-values in Col B.
2. Select Calc > Regression > Quadratic Regression (mx^2+nx+p).
3. Note the values for a, b, c.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [STAT] > Edit.
2. Enter x-values {1, 5, 9} in L1.
3. Enter y-values {0.5+ln(2), 1.5+ln(11), ln(3)} in L2.
4. Press [STAT] > CALC > 5:QuadReg.
5. Store RegEQ if needed, or note values.

desmos.instructions: Enter the table of values for (x1, y1). Then type 'y1 ~ ax1^2 + bx1 + c' to find parameters.

calculator.description: Find the quadratic coefficients a, b, c

steps.title: State the coefficients
steps.content:
From the calculator regression, we find the coefficients:

$$\begin{aligned} a &\approx -0.172 \\ b &\approx 1.708 \\ c &\approx -0.343 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate h (x-coordinate of vertex)
steps.content:
Use the axis of symmetry formula from the data booklet to find $h$. This formula gives the $x$-coordinate of the vertex.

$$x = -\frac{b}{2a}$$

Substituting our values:

$$h = -\frac{1.708}{2(-0.172)} \approx 4.966$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate -b/(2a).

ti84.instructions: Calculate -b/(2a) using the stored values or typed approximations.

desmos.instructions: Type '-b/(2a)' after finding regression parameters.

calculator.description: Calculate h

steps.title: Calculate k (y-coordinate of vertex)
steps.content:
To find $k$, substitute $h$ back into the function $f(x) = ax^2 + bx + c$:

$$\begin{aligned} k &= f(4.966) \\ k &= -0.172(4.966)^2 + 1.708(4.966) - 0.343 \\ k &\approx 3.898 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate f(h).

ti84.instructions: Calculate the function value at h: a*h^2 + b*h + c

desmos.instructions: Evaluate the function at the calculated h.

calculator.description: Calculate k

steps.title: Write vertex form and vertex
steps.content:
The vertex form is $f(x) = a(x-h)^2 + k$. Substituting the values correct to 3 decimal places as requested:

$$f(x) = -0.172(x - 4.966)^2 + 3.898$$

The vertex is $(h, k)$:

$$\text{Vertex} = (4.966, 3.898)$$

steps.title: Determine the range
steps.content:
Since $a \approx -0.172$ is negative, the parabola opens downwards. This means the vertex represents the maximum point.

The range is all real values less than or equal to the maximum $y$-value ($k$):

$$\text{Range}: (-\infty, 3.898]$$

steps.title: Determine the quadratic model
steps.content:
First, use your GDC to find the coefficients $a$, $b$, and $c$ for the model $f(x)=ax^2+bx+c$ by performing a quadratic regression on the three given points.

Input the points $P(1, 0.5+\ln 2)$, $Q(5, 1.5+\ln 11)$, and $R(9, \ln 3)$ into your calculator's lists:
- $x$-values: $1, 5, 9$
- $y$-values: calculate $0.5+\ln 2 \approx 1.193$, $1.5+\ln 11 \approx 3.898$, and $\ln 3 \approx 1.099$

Perform a **Quadratic Regression** to get:
$$f(x) \approx -0.172x^2 + 1.708x - 0.343$$

gdc.instructions:
1. In 'Lists & Spreadsheet', name columns 'x_vals' and 'y_vals'.
2. Enter data.
3. Menu > Statistics > Stat Calculations > Quadratic Regression.
4. Select 'x_vals' and 'y_vals'.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [STAT] > Edit.
2. Enter x-values into L1: 1, 5, 9.
3. Enter y-values into L2: 0.5+ln(2), 1.5+ln(11), ln(3).
4. Press [STAT] > CALC > 5:QuadReg.
5. Note a ≈ -0.172, b ≈ 1.708.

desmos.instructions:
1. Add a table and enter the points.
2. Type 'y1 ~ ax1^2 + bx1 + c'.
3. Note parameters a = -0.172, b = 1.708.

calculator.description: Find quadratic model coefficients

steps.title: Find vertex using GDC
steps.content:
Graph the function $f(x) = -0.172x^2 + 1.708x - 0.343$ on your GDC. Since the coefficient of $x^2$ is negative ($a < 0$), the parabola opens downwards, so the vertex is a **maximum**.

Use the **Analyze Graph** tool to find the coordinates of this maximum point $(h, k)$.

- The $x$-coordinate is the value of $h$.
- The $y$-coordinate is the value of $k$.

gdc.instructions:
1. In 'Graph', enter f1(x) = -0.172x^2 + 1.708x - 0.343.
2. Menu > Analyze Graph > Maximum.
3. Click bounds around the vertex.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [Y=] and enter -0.172x^2 + 1.708x - 0.343.
2. Press [GRAPH]. (Adjust [WINDOW] if needed).
3. Press [2nd] [TRACE] (CALC) > 4:Maximum.
4. Set Left/Right bounds and Guess.

desmos.instructions: Click on the vertex point of the parabola to see coordinates.

calculator.description: Find the vertex (maximum) of the graph

steps.title: State h and k
steps.content:
From the calculator analysis, the coordinates of the maximum are approximately $(4.966, 3.898)$.

Therefore, correct to 3 decimal places:
$$\begin{aligned} h &= 4.966 \\ k &= 3.898 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Write vertex form
steps.content:
Substitute $a \approx -0.172$, $h = 4.966$, and $k = 3.898$ into the vertex form $f(x) = a(x-h)^2 + k$.

$$f(x) = -0.172(x-4.966)^2 + 3.898$$

steps.title: State vertex and range
steps.content:
The vertex is simply the point $(h, k)$. Since the parabola opens downwards ($a < 0$), the function takes all values less than or equal to the maximum $y$-value, $k$.

**Vertex:** $(4.966, 3.898)$

**Range:** $(-\infty, 3.898]$

steps.title: Retrieve coefficients
steps.content:
First, we use the coefficients $a$, $b$, and $c$ determined in Part 1. To ensure our final calculation is accurate to 3 decimal places, we will use values with higher precision for the intermediate steps.

$$a \approx -0.17200$$
$$b \approx 1.70819$$
$$c \approx -0.34304$$

The standard equation is:
$$f(x) = -0.17200x^2 + 1.70819x - 0.34304$$

steps.title: Factor out a
steps.content:
To complete the square, factor out $a$ ($-0.17200$) from the $x^2$ and $x$ terms.

$$f(x) = -0.17200\left(x^2 + \frac{1.70819}{-0.17200}x\right) - 0.34304$$

Perform the division inside the parentheses:

$$f(x) = -0.17200(x^2 - 9.93134x) - 0.34304$$

calculator.description: Calculate the coefficient of x inside the parentheses

steps.title: Find magic number
steps.content:
Take half of the $x$-coefficient ($-9.93134$) and square it to find the value needed to complete the square.

$$\text{Half: } \frac{-9.93134}{2} = -4.96567$$
$$\text{Square: } (-4.96567)^2 \approx 24.65788$$

Add and subtract this value inside the parentheses:

$$f(x) = -0.17200(x^2 - 9.93134x + 24.65788 - 24.65788) - 0.34304$$

steps.title: Form the square
steps.content:
Convert the first three terms inside the parentheses into a perfect square $(x - 4.96567)^2$. Leave the subtracted term inside for now.

$$f(x) = -0.17200\left[ (x - 4.96567)^2 - 24.65788 \right] - 0.34304$$

steps.title: Distribute and simplify
steps.content:
Distribute the outer coefficient $-0.17200$ to the terms inside the square brackets to solve for $k$.

$$\begin{aligned} f(x) &= -0.17200(x - 4.96567)^2 + (-0.17200)(-24.65788) - 0.34304 \\ &= -0.17200(x - 4.96567)^2 + 4.24116 - 0.34304 \\ &= -0.17200(x - 4.96567)^2 + 3.89812 \end{aligned}$$

Rounding $h$ and $k$ to 3 decimal places as requested:

$$f(x) = -0.172(x - 4.966)^2 + 3.898$$

calculator.description: Calculate the final constant k

steps.title: State vertex and range
steps.content:
From the vertex form $f(x) = a(x-h)^2 + k$, the vertex is $(h, k)$.

**Vertex:** $(4.966, 3.898)$

Since $a$ is negative ($-0.172$), the parabola opens downwards. This means the vertex represents the maximum value, and the range is all real numbers up to and including $k$.

**Range:** $(-\infty, 3.898]$

parts.content: Let \(g(x)=\ln(x+2)+1\). Solve \(f(x)=g(x)\) for \(x\ge 0\). Give your answers correct to 3 decimal places, and justify the number of solutions.
parts.markscheme:
- \(F(x)=ax^2+bx+c-(\ln(x+2)+1)\). <marks>M1</marks>
- Roots: \(x\approx 2.001\) and \(x\approx 6.986\). <marks>M1</marks><marks>A1</marks>
- \(g\) is increasing; \(f\) is concave down; \(F(0)<0,\ F(h)>0,\ \lim_{x\to\infty} F(x)=-\infty\). Two solutions. <marks>R1</marks><marks>R1</marks>

steps.title: Enter data for f(x)
steps.content:
Since $f(x) = ax^2+bx+c$ is fitted to three specific points, we use the **quadratic regression** feature on the GDC to determine the function. We do not need to solve for $a, b, c$ manually.

Calculate the $y$-values for the points $P, Q, R$:
- $y_1 = 0.5 + \ln(2) \approx 1.193$
- $y_2 = 1.5 + \ln(11) \approx 3.898$
- $y_3 = \ln(3) \approx 1.099$

Enter the $x$-coordinates $\{1, 5, 9\}$ into List 1 and the calculated $y$-values into List 2.

gdc.instructions: Enter points in spreadsheet. Select data > Menu > Statistics > Stat Calculations > Quadratic Regression.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [STAT] > Edit.
2. Enter x-values {1, 5, 9} in L1.
3. Enter y-values {0.5+ln(2), 1.5+ln(11), ln(3)} in L2.
4. Press [STAT] > CALC > 5:QuadReg.
5. Store RegEQ in Y1 (using [VARS] > Y-VARS).

desmos.instructions: Create a table with the points. Type regression equation: y1 ~ ax1^2 + bx1 + c

calculator.description: Perform Quadratic Regression to store f(x)

steps.title: Plot both functions
steps.content:
We need to solve $f(x) = g(x)$. Plot the quadratic function $f(x)$ (which is now stored in your calculator) and the logarithmic function $g(x) = \ln(x+2)+1$ on the same graph.

Adjust the window settings to see the intersection points. A good range for $x$ is $0$ to $10$ (since our data points go up to $9$).

gdc.instructions: In Graph app, verify f1(x) is the quadratic. Add f2(x) = ln(x+2)+1. Draw both.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [Y=].
2. Y1 should already contain the regression equation.
3. In Y2, enter ln(X+2)+1.
4. Press [WINDOW]. Set Xmin=0, Xmax=10, Ymin=-2, Ymax=5.
5. Press [GRAPH].

desmos.instructions: Type g(x) = ln(x+2)+1. Ensure both graphs are visible.

calculator.description: Graph the functions

steps.title: Find intersection points
steps.content: Use the intersection tool on your GDC to find the $x$-coordinates where the two graphs cross. There should be two points of intersection.

gdc.instructions: Menu > Analyze Graph > Intersection. Select lower and upper bounds for each point.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [2nd] [TRACE] (CALC).
2. Select 5:intersect.
3. Move cursor near the first crossing, press [ENTER] three times.
4. Repeat for the second crossing.

desmos.instructions: Click directly on the intersection points to reveal their coordinates.

calculator.description: Find coordinates of intersection

steps.title: State the solutions
steps.content:
The calculator gives the intersection $x$-values. Round them to 3 decimal places as required.

First intersection: $x \approx 2.001$
Second intersection: $x \approx 6.986$

steps.title: Justify number of solutions
steps.content:
To justify the number of solutions, sketch the shapes of the two functions:
1.  $f(x)$ is a **concave down** parabola (it goes up and comes back down).
2.  $g(x)$ is an **increasing** logarithmic function (it goes up but gets flatter).

The parabola rises above the logarithmic curve and then falls back below it. Therefore, the graphs must cross exactly twice.

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
The solutions for $f(x)=g(x)$ are:
$$x = 2.001, \quad x = 6.986$$

steps.title: Define the difference function
steps.content:
To solve $f(x) = g(x)$ using the **Roots of Difference Function** approach, we define a new function $h(x)$ that represents the difference between the two functions:

$$h(x) = f(x) - g(x) = ax^2+bx+c - (\ln(x+2)+1)$$

The solutions to $f(x)=g(x)$ correspond to the roots (zeros) of $h(x)$.

steps.title: Find the quadratic model
steps.content:
First, determine the coefficients $a, b,$ and $c$ for $f(x)$. Enter the three given points $P(1, 0.5+\ln 2)$, $Q(5, 1.5+\ln 11)$, and $R(9, \ln 3)$ into your GDC and perform a **Quadratic Regression** to find the function $f(x)$. Store this function into $Y_1$.

From the regression, you will find:
$$f(x) \approx -0.172x^2 + 1.708x - 0.343$$

gdc.instructions: In Statistics mode, enter the lists of points. Select Calc > Regression > Quadratic (x^2) and copy the formula to the Graph list.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [STAT] > Edit.
2. Enter x-values {1, 5, 9} in L1 and y-values {0.5+ln(2), 1.5+ln(11), ln(3)} in L2.
3. Press [STAT] > CALC > 5:QuadReg.
4. Store RegEQ in Y1.

desmos.instructions: Create a table with the points. Type 'y1 ~ ax1^2 + bx1 + c' to find parameters a, b, c.

calculator.description: Find the quadratic equation through the points

steps.title: Graph the difference function
steps.content:
Enter $g(x) = \ln(x+2)+1$ into $Y_2$. Then, define the difference function in $Y_3$:

$$Y_3 = Y_1 - Y_2$$

Plot $Y_3$ on your calculator for $x \ge 0$. You are looking for the $x$-intercepts where the graph crosses the horizontal axis.

gdc.instructions: Define f1(x) as the quadratic and f2(x) as ln(x+2)+1. Plot f3(x) = f1(x) - f2(x).

ti84.instructions:
1. In Y= menu, ensure Y1 is your quadratic.
2. Set Y2 = ln(x+2)+1.
3. Set Y3 = Y1 - Y2.
4. Disable Y1 and Y2 (unhighlight =).
5. Graph Y3 in a window like X[0, 10], Y[-5, 5].

desmos.instructions: Define f(x) with the found parameters. Define g(x)=ln(x+2)+1. Plot h(x)=f(x)-g(x).

calculator.description: Graph the difference function h(x)

steps.title: Calculate the roots
steps.content:
Use the root/zero finding feature on your GDC to find the $x$-values where $h(x)=0$. 

The graph crosses the x-axis at two points:
$$x \approx 2.001$$
$$x \approx 6.986$$

gdc.instructions: Use G-Solve > Root to find the x-intercepts of the difference function.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [2nd] [TRACE] (CALC).
2. Select 2: zero.
3. Set Left Bound and Right Bound around each intersection.
4. Repeat for both roots.

desmos.instructions: Click on the intersection points of h(x) with the x-axis to see the coordinates.

calculator.description: Find the zeros of h(x)

steps.title: Justify the number of solutions
steps.content:
To justify there are exactly two solutions, we analyze the behavior of $h(x) = f(x) - g(x)$:

1.  **Start point:** At $x=0$, $f(0) \approx -0.34$ and $g(0) \approx 1.69$, so $h(0) < 0$.
2.  **Peak region:** Around $x=5$ (near the vertex of $f$), $f(5) \approx 3.9$ and $g(5) \approx 2.9$, so $h(5) > 0$. This confirms the graph crosses from negative to positive (first solution).
3.  **End behavior:** As $x \to \infty$, the quadratic term $-ax^2$ (with $a<0$) dominates the logarithmic term, so $h(x) \to -\infty$. The graph must cross back from positive to negative (second solution).

Since $f$ is concave down and $g$ is increasing, the difference function has a single maximum and crosses the axis exactly twice.

parts.content: Solve \(f(x)\ge g(x)\) for \(x\ge 0\).
parts.markscheme:
- Equalities at \(2.001,\,6.986\). <marks>M1</marks>
- Midpoint test \(F>0\). <marks>A1</marks>
- \([2.001,\,6.986]\). <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the task
steps.content:
The question asks us to solve the inequality $$f(x) \ge g(x)$$ for $$x \ge 0$$. 

We need to find the range of $x$-values where the graph of the quadratic model $f(x)$ is above or equal to the graph of $g(x)$. We will use the **Graphical Intersection** method on the GDC (Graphing Display Calculator) to find the points of intersection and identify the correct interval.

steps.title: Plot the functions
steps.content:
First, enter the equation for the quadratic model $f(x)$ into your calculator. Based on the three given points $P$, $Q$, and $R$, the quadratic function is approximately:

$$f(x) \approx -0.172x^2 + 1.708x - 0.343$$

Enter this into $Y_1$. Then, enter the function $g(x)$ (which would have been defined in a previous part of the question) into $Y_2$.

Adjust your window settings to ensure you can see the interval $x \ge 0$ and the relevant $y$-values (around $y=0$ to $y=5$).

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to Graph mode.
2. Input f(x) and g(x) as Y1 and Y2.
3. Draw the graphs.
4. Use G-Solv > ISCT (Intersection) to find the coordinates of points where the graphs meet.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [Y=].
2. Enter f(x) into Y1.
3. Enter g(x) into Y2.
4. Press [GRAPH].
5. Press [2nd] [TRACE] (CALC) and select 5: intersect.
6. Select First curve, Second curve, and Guess near the first intersection.
7. Repeat for the second intersection.

desmos.instructions:
1. Type f(x) = -0.172x^2 + 1.708x - 0.343.
2. Type g(x) = ...
3. Click the points where the curves cross to see coordinates.

calculator.description: Plotting functions and finding intersections

steps.title: Find intersection points
steps.content:
Use the intersection tool on your GDC to find the exact $x$-coordinates where the two curves meet ($f(x) = g(x)$).

The calculator will give two intersection values:
$$x_1 \approx 2.001$$
$$x_2 \approx 6.986$$

steps.title: Determine the interval
steps.content:
We need to solve for $$f(x) \ge g(x)$$, which corresponds to the region where the graph of $f(x)$ is **above** the graph of $g(x)$.

Looking at the graph, $f(x)$ (the concave down parabola) lies above $g(x)$ in the interval between the two intersection points. You can verify this by checking a midpoint value (e.g., at $x=4.5$) to see that $f(4.5) > g(4.5)$.

Therefore, the solution is the closed interval between the roots.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The inequality holds for:

$$2.001 \le x \le 6.986$$

Or in interval notation: $$[2.001, 6.986]$$

steps.title: Define the difference function
steps.content:
To solve the inequality $f(x) \ge g(x)$ using the **Zeros of Difference Function** approach, we first rearrange the inequality so that one side is zero. This allows us to focus on finding the roots of a single function.

Subtract $g(x)$ from both sides:
$$f(x) - g(x) \ge 0$$

Let's define a difference function $h(x) = f(x) - g(x)$. We are now looking for the values of $x$ where $h(x) \ge 0$.

steps.title: Find the zeros using GDC
steps.content:
Use your graphing calculator to find the zeros (roots) of the difference function $h(x)$. These are the critical values where the inequality could change from true to false.

Enter the equation $y = f(x) - g(x)$ into your calculator and use the root-finding feature to identify the $x$-intercepts.

According to the markscheme, the difference function is zero at:
$$x \approx 2.001 \quad \text{and} \quad x \approx 6.986$$

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to Graph menu.
2. Enter \\y1 = f(x) - g(x)\\.
3. Draw the graph.
4. Select G-Solve (or Analysis) > Root (or Zero).
5. The calculator will display the x-values where the graph crosses the x-axis.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [Y=].
2. In \\Y_1\\, enter the expression for f(x).
3. In \\Y_2\\, enter the expression for g(x).
4. In \\Y_3\\, enter \\Y_1 - Y_2\\.
5. Press [GRAPH]. (You may deselect Y1 and Y2 to see only the difference).
6. Press [2nd] [TRACE] (CALC) and select 2:Zero.
7. Use the cursor to set Left Bound, Right Bound, and Guess for each intersection.

desmos.instructions:
1. Enter f(x) = [expression].
2. Enter g(x) = [expression].
3. Type f(x) - g(x) = 0 to see vertical lines at the roots, or graph y = f(x) - g(x) and click the x-intercepts.

calculator.description: Find zeros of the difference function

steps.title: Test the intervals
steps.content:
These roots divide the domain $x \ge 0$ into three intervals:
1. $[0, 2.001]$
2. $[2.001, 6.986]$
3. $[6.986, \infty)$

We need to check which interval satisfies $h(x) \ge 0$. You can test a value between the roots, such as $x=5$, or simply observe the graph. Since $f(x)$ is a quadratic fitted to a peak in the middle (points $P, Q, R$), the difference $f(x) - g(x)$ is positive between the two intersection points.

The markscheme confirms the "Midpoint test $F > 0$", meaning the inequality holds in the middle interval.

steps.title: State the final solution
steps.content:
The inequality $f(x) \ge g(x)$ is satisfied for values of $x$ between the two roots.

Therefore, the solution set is:
$$2.001 \le x \le 6.986$$

Or in interval notation:
$$[2.001, 6.986]$$

questions.specification: A quadratic has roots $x=-7$ and $x=1$; its vertex lies on $y=4$.

parts.content: Find $f(x)$.
parts.markscheme:
- Attempt to express $f(x)$ using roots: $f(x)=a(x+7)(x-1)=a[(x+3)^2-16]=a(x+3)^2-16a$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Use vertex property: Vertex $y$-coordinate is $-16a$, so $-16a=4$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Solve for $a$: $a=-\frac{1}{4}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Substitute $a$ into the equation: $f(x)=-\frac{1}{4}(x+7)(x-1)$ or $f(x)=-\frac{1}{4}(x+3)^2+4$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Correct final expression for $f(x)$ <marks>A1</marks>

5 marks total

steps.title: Set up factored form
steps.content:
Since we are given the roots of the quadratic, we start with the factored form equation:

$$f(x) = a(x - p)(x - q)$$

where $p$ and $q$ are the roots (x-intercepts).

steps.title: Substitute the roots
steps.content:
Substitute the given roots $x = -7$ and $x = 1$ into the equation:

$$f(x) = a(x - (-7))(x - 1)$$

$$f(x) = a(x + 7)(x - 1)$$

steps.title: Find axis of symmetry
steps.content:
The axis of symmetry lies exactly in the middle of the two roots. We calculate the $x$-coordinate of the vertex by finding the midpoint:

$$x_{\text{vertex}} = \frac{-7 + 1}{2} = \frac{-6}{2} = -3$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate (-7 + 1) / 2

ti84.instructions: Calculate (-7 + 1) / 2

calculator.description: Calculate the midpoint of the roots

steps.title: Evaluate at the vertex
steps.content:
Substitute $x = -3$ into our equation to find the corresponding $y$-value in terms of $a$:

$$\begin{aligned} f(-3) &= a(-3 + 7)(-3 - 1) \\ &= a(4)(-4) \\ &= -16a \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate (-3 + 7) * (-3 - 1)

ti84.instructions: Calculate (-3 + 7) * (-3 - 1)

calculator.description: Verify the product of the factors

steps.title: Solve for a
steps.content:
The question states that the vertex lies on $y = 4$. We equate our expression to this value and solve for the vertical stretch factor $a$:

$$\begin{aligned} -16a &= 4 \\ a &= \frac{4}{-16} \\ a &= -\frac{1}{4} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: State the final function
steps.content:
Now substitute $a = -\frac{1}{4}$ back into the factored form equation from Step 1:

$$f(x) = -\frac{1}{4}(x + 7)(x - 1)$$

steps.title: Find the x-coordinate of the vertex
steps.content:
Since a quadratic function is symmetric, the $x$-coordinate of the vertex (often denoted as $h$) lies exactly in the middle of the two roots.

We calculate the midpoint of the roots $x = -7$ and $x = 1$:

$$h = \frac{-7 + 1}{2}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate (-7+1)/2

ti84.instructions: Calculate (-7+1)/2

desmos.instructions: Calculate (-7+1)/2

calculator.description: Calculate the midpoint of the roots

steps.title: State the vertex coordinates
steps.content:
Calculating the value from the previous step:

$$h = -3$$

The question states the vertex lies on $y=4$. Therefore, the vertex coordinates $(h, k)$ are $(-3, 4)$.

steps.title: Write the vertex form equation
steps.content:
The vertex form of a quadratic function is given by:

$$f(x) = a(x - h)^2 + k$$

Substituting our vertex $(h, k) = (-3, 4)$ into the equation:

$$f(x) = a(x - (-3))^2 + 4$$

$$f(x) = a(x + 3)^2 + 4$$

steps.title: Substitute a root to find 'a'
steps.content:
To find the scaling factor $a$, we substitute one of the known roots into the equation. Let's use the root $x = 1$. Since it is a root, the function value $f(1) = 0$.

$$0 = a(1 + 3)^2 + 4$$

steps.title: Solve for 'a'
steps.content:
Now we solve the equation for $a$:

$$\begin{aligned} 0 &= a(4)^2 + 4 \\ 0 &= 16a + 4 \\ -4 &= 16a \\ a &= -\frac{4}{16} \\ a &= -\frac{1}{4} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Solve -4/16

ti84.instructions: Solve -4/16

desmos.instructions: Solve -4/16

calculator.description: Verify the calculation for a

steps.title: Write the final function
steps.content:
Substitute $a = -\frac{1}{4}$ back into the vertex form equation to get the final answer:

$$f(x) = -\frac{1}{4}(x + 3)^2 + 4$$

This matches the form given in the markscheme.

parts.content: Determine the range of $f(x)$.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify shape and maximum value: Parabola opens downwards with maximum $4$ <marks>M1</marks>
- State the range: $(-\infty, 4]$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Analyze the y-values
steps.content:
First, we compare the vertical position of the vertex with the roots to determine the shape of the graph.

*   **Roots:** The question states the roots are $x=-7$ and $x=1$. Roots are $x$-intercepts, so their $y$-value is always $0$.
*   **Vertex:** The question states the vertex lies on $y=4$.

We observe that the vertex ($y=4$) is located **above** the roots ($y=0$).

steps.title: Deduce the parabola's shape
steps.content:
Since the vertex is the turning point at $y=4$ (above the axis) and the graph must curve to pass through the roots at $y=0$ (on the axis), the parabola must open **downwards**.

If the parabola opened upwards from $y=4$, it would go towards positive infinity and never cross the x-axis to form roots.

Because it opens downwards, the vertex represents the **maximum** value of the function.

$$ \text{Maximum value} = 4 $$

steps.title: Verify with a graph
steps.content:
We can verify this conceptual deduction by visualizing the key points on a graphing calculator. The axis of symmetry is the midpoint of the roots ($x=-3$), so the vertex is at $(-3, 4)$.

Plotting the roots $(-7, 0)$ and $(1, 0)$ along with the vertex $(-3, 4)$ clearly shows a downward curve.

gdc.instructions: Enter the coordinates in the Statistics/List app and plot a scatter graph.

ti84.instructions: Use the Stat Plot feature to plot the three known points.

desmos.instructions: Enter the points directly to see their relative positions.

calculator.description: Visualize the key points to confirm the shape

steps.title: State the range
steps.content:
The range is the set of all possible $y$-values. Since the maximum value is $4$ and the parabola extends downwards to negative infinity, $f(x)$ must be less than or equal to $4$.

$$ f(x) \leq 4 $$

In interval notation, this is written as:

$$ (-\infty, 4] $$

This matches the markscheme requirement to identify the maximum of 4 and state the correct interval.

steps.title: Set up factored form
steps.content:
Since the roots are given as $x=-7$ and $x=1$, we can express the quadratic function in factored form:

$$f(x) = a(x - r_1)(x - r_2)$$

Substituting the roots:

$$f(x) = a(x - (-7))(x - 1) = a(x + 7)(x - 1)$$

steps.title: Find axis of symmetry
steps.content:
The $x$-coordinate of the vertex lies exactly in the middle of the two roots. We calculate the midpoint using the axis of symmetry formula:

$$x = \frac{-7 + 1}{2} = -3$$

So, the vertex is at $(-3, 4)$.

steps.title: Solve for leading coefficient
steps.content:
Now we substitute the vertex coordinates $x=-3$ and $f(x)=4$ into our equation to solve for $a$:

$$\begin{aligned} 4 &= a(-3 + 7)(-3 - 1) \\ 4 &= a(4)(-4) \\ 4 &= -16a \\ a &= -\frac{1}{4} \end{aligned}$$

Since $a$ is negative ($a < 0$), the parabola opens downwards.
